pimpQuestions: Q: What is the most common surgical treatment for subtrochanteric femur fractures? A: Intramedullary nail
Q: What are the non-arthroplasty surgical treatment options for IT fractures? A: 1) Sliding hip screw (SHS) 2) Cephalomedullary nail (CMN)
Q: What makes an IT fracture unstable? A: 1) Reverse obliquity 2) Fracture extension into the subtrochanteric region 3) Disrupted medial calcar 4) Disruption of the lateral cortex
Q: What is the advantage of placing an intramedullary nail with the patient in the lateral decubitus position? A: Allows for easier reduction of fracture fragments
Q: What is the most common malunion following antegrade IMN for subtrochanteric femur fractures? A: Varus and procurvatum

Q: What are the deforming forces on a subtrochanteric femur fracture? A: Proximal fragment: Abduction - Gluteus medius/minimus; Flexion - Iliopsoas; External rotation - Short external rotators; Distal fragment: Adduction and shortening - Adductors
Q: What radiograph should be obtained to better delineate the fracture pattern in intertrochanteric femur fractures? A: Traction view
Q: What additional radiograph must be obtained preoperatively for intertrochanteric femur fractures? A: Full length femur x-ray
Q: What is the importance of the TAD? A: TAD >25mm is associated with screw cutout
otherUsefulFacts: Sliding hip screws should be avoided in unstable fracture patterns.
IT fractures have better healing potential because they are extracapsular, have periosteum, and soft tissue attachment.

selected.rationale: Hardinge (lateral hip) approach provides direct access to the proximal femur and greater trochanter region, ideal for intramedullary fixation of intertrochanteric fractures.

approachSelection.notes: Hardinge approach is preferred over lateral thigh approach for intertrochanteric fractures due to better proximal femur exposure.

questions.q: Which superficial interval is split in the Hardinge lateral approach to the hip?
In the Hardinge approach, which two muscles are divided in the deep interval to expose the hip joint?
Which nerve is at risk during the Hardinge lateral approach to the hip?
Which arterial branch is at risk during the Hardinge approach?
questions.answer: Transverse branch of the lateral femoral circumflex artery (LFCA)

questions.q: The Hardinge approach provides surgical access primarily to which joint?
The Hardinge approach to the hip involves splitting which deep interval?
questions.answer: Between gluteus medius and vastus lateralis

questions.q: What muscle group's tendon is involved in the superficial interval during the Hardinge approach?
questions.answer: Iliotibial band (tensor fasciae latae muscle's tendon)

questions.q: Name two bones primarily exposed during the Hardinge approach.
